data.title: Growth vs. Value had its best two-year run since 1978 – BofA
data.description:
The Russell 1000 Growth Index (NYSEARCA:IWF) outperformed the Russell 1000 Value Index (IWD) by 63 percentage points over two years, up 90% vs. 27%, after leading by 19 percentage points in 2024, up 33% vs. 14%.

“This represents the biggest two-year outperformance for Growth since the indices’ inception in 1978, topping the 61 percentage points outperformance during the Tech Bubble,” said Savita Subramanian, head of U.S. Equity Strategy and U.S. Quantitative Strategy at BofA, in a note.

In addition, the month of December was a particularly strong month for Growth (IWF), outperforming Value (IWD) by 7.7 percentage points, the best performance since May 2023.

The S&P 500 (SP500) was up 25% in 2024 and up 58% over the past two years. However, it dropped 2.4% in December – its worst month since April.

The equal-weighted S&P 500 (RSP) was up 12% this year, and up 28% over the last two years. It had two of the weakest breadth measures in history, with 30% of stocks outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) in 2024 and 27% of stocks outperforming in 2023.

data.title: Wall Street shakes off recent weakness, Nasdaq surges more than 1% on tech boost
data.description:
U.S. stocks on Friday surged, as Wall Street finally appeared to shake off the doldrums afflicting it this week. A dull end to 2024 and a dour start to 2025 derailed the Santa Claus rally, and the three major averages were set for weekly losses of nearly 1% each.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +1.00% at 5,927.45 points in midday trade. The gauge was on track to snap a five-day losing streak, its longest such negative run since April.

Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.59% at 42,644.32 points, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.31% at 19,533.98 points.

All 11 S&P sectors were in the green, led by Consumer Discretionary and Utilities.

Technology was +1.3%, as every single member of the Magnificent 7 club climbed - except Apple (AAPL). Reuters earlier reported that sales of foreign-branded smartphones in China tumbled 47.4% Y/Y in November, citing government data. Apple (AAPL) is the largest foreign smartphone manufacturer in China.

Over in the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields were mixed. The 10-year yield (US10Y) was up 2 basis points to 4.59%, while the shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) was marginally down to 4.26%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

Despite the first trading session of the new year ending in the red on Thursday, investors should take heart from the fact that the S&P 500's (SP500) performance on the first trading day of a year has historically been a poor barometer for eventual annual returns. See the chart here.

The spotlight on Friday was captured by a collision between the financial world and politics. Shares of U. S. Steel (X) were -5.6%, after U.S. President Joe Biden officially blocked the proposed $14.9B sale of the iconic 123-year-old American steel giant to Japan's Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY)(OTCPK:NISTF). The President cited national security concerns for his decision.

Beer and alcohol companies also saw some notable stock declines, after the U.S. Surgeon General urged for the addition of cancer warning labels to alcoholic drinks.

Turning to Friday's economic calendar, the Institute for Supply Management said economic activity in the U.S. manufacturing sector improved slightly in December from November, though still staying in contraction for the ninth straight month.

"Few things were as consistent as the ISM Manufacturing Index in the past year. The index has been in contraction for the past nine-straight months and for 25 of the past 26. Uncertainty continues to hold back broad activity, though there are some signs of stabilizing demand amid industries," Wells Fargo's Tim Quinlan said.

"Late last year we heard from manufacturing clients that they were planning for a 2025 similar to 2024, where this persistent uncertainty plagues new investment. But there isn't much sign of this caution, or tariff-anxiety, in today's report. Despite the index remaining in contraction to end the year, the details were fairly positive and consistent with some recent improvement," Quinlan added.

data.title: Nvidia expands reach as most widely owned semi among fund managers: BofA
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) expanded its reach as the most widely owned semiconductor stock among active US fund managers, according to an industry update by Bank of America.

As of the end of December 2024, 72% of fund managers owned Nvidia. This was up from 70% at the end of September.

The next most owned semis included Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) at 54.6%, Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) and Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) both at 35.7%, Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) at 30.5% and Texas Instruments (TXN) at 30%.

"Notably, despite confidence in NVDA's long-term growth prospects, the stock's weighting remains relatively low at 1.01x, up slightly from the 0.99x level cited in the last update (Sep ’24), but down from 1.03x in Dec '23," said BofA analysts, led by Vivek Arya.

The data showed the largest ownership expansions quarter over quarter included Nvidia +2%, Broadcom +2.4%, ON Semiconductor (NASDAQ:ON) +0.4% and Cadence Design Systems (NASDAQ:CDNS) +0.2%. The largest quarter-over-quarter declines in ownership were Applied Materials -4.4%, Lam Research (LRCX) -4%, Qualcomm -3%, AMD -2.8% and Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) -2.6%.

"Notably, Intel at 8.4% ownership is held well below $50bn+ market-cap-peers ranging 15% - 70% ownership," Arya noted.

The overall percentage of semiconductor and semicap equipment stocks held by fund managers fell quarter over quarter from 24.7% at the end of September to 23% by the end of December.

data.title: Wells Fargo’s Harvey: Expect choppy trades, small- and mid-caps to correct
data.description:
Small-caps (SP600) and mid-caps (SP400) are “reaching a correction territory” with the jump in the 10-year real rates, Wells Fargo analysts said.

Equities saw weakness around Dec. 6 through 19 as the 10-year rates surged from 1.9% to 2.27%. The S&P 500 (SP500) fell 3.6% in nine trading days, mid-caps (SP400) were down 6.7%, and small-caps (SP600) were down 7.7%.

“This reaction did not surprise us,” said Christopher P. Harvey, head of equity strategy at Wells Fargo. “The jump in 10-year real rates is more restrictive, so risk, cyclicality, and leverage are all expected to sell off.”

The 10-year breakevens – the expected inflation coming from 10-year Treasury Constant Maturity Securities and 10-Year Treasury Inflation-Indexed Constant Maturity Securities – rose about 4-5 basis points despite the inflation worries.

Also, the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) is “searching for a top,” said Harvey. “And will likely find one in the first quarter of 2025.”

He said that the 10-year real yields are comparable to the levels seen during the first quarter of 2006, when monetary policy was tightening.

“In the last 20 years, 10-year breakevens (now 2.35%) have been more than 2.5%, only slightly more than 10% of the time,” he said.

Harvey said 2.2% 10-year real yields and stable break-evens “create an attractive risk/reward and will attract buyers and ‘firm up’ yields.”

He expects equities (SP500) to trade “choppy” until then, and the mid- (SP400) and small-caps (SP600) to reach a correction territory.

Wells Fargo analysts still see the S&P 500 (SP500) year-end target for 2025 at 7,007.

data.title: Booz Allen to pay $15.9M to settle DoD False Claims Act allegations
data.description:
Booz Allen Hamilton (NYSE:BAH) +2.4% in Friday's trading after the U.S. Department of Justice said the company agreed to pay $15.875M to resolve False Claims Act allegations.

The DoJ said Booz Allen (BAH) had knowingly submitted fraudulent claims related to a General Services Administration task order to supply computer military training simulators and systems to Department of Defense agencies, including the Air Force.

The DoJ alleged a civilian Air Force employee and contracting official improperly and illegally divulged confidential government contracting and budget information, and competitor's confidential bid and proposal information to two former Booz Allen (BAH) program managers who used the illicit information despite knowing they were not authorized to possess it; through the conduct, the two program managers successfully influenced GSA to award the task order to Booz Allen.

data.title: The S&P 500's 10 worst-performing stocks in 2023 fell again in 2024
data.description:
While the S&P 500’s (SP500) surge in 2024 logged a milestone of annual gains not seen in nearly 30 years, some of the index’s sharpest lagging stocks notched another annual decline.

The index (SP500) this week finished 2024 by closing up +23%, topping up on its +24% leap in 2023. That feat marked the first time since 1997-1998 the benchmark finished with back-to-back annual increases of more than +20%.

But a “crazy stat” stands out from the S&P 500’s (SP500) consecutive +20% jumps: the 10 worst-performing stocks in 2023 “were all down again in 2024,” Bespoke Investment Group noted in a post on X (formerly Twitter) on Thursday.

Here’s its list of those 10 S&P 500 (SP500) stocks:

Enphase Energy (NASDAQ:ENPH): 2024 loss: -48%. 2023 drop: -50.1%

FMC (NYSE:FMC): 2024 loss: -22.9%. 2023 drop: -49.5%

Dollar General (NYSE:DG): 2024 loss: -44.2%. 2023 drop: -44.8%

Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA): 2024 loss: -58.2%. 2023 drop: -44.6%

Pfizer (NYSE:PFE): 2024 loss: -7.9%. 2023 drop: -43.8%

Estee Lauder (NYSE:EL): 2024 loss: -48.7%. 2023 drop: -41.1%

Paycom (NYSE:PAYC): 2024 loss: -0.85%. 2023 drop: -33.4%

Albemarle (NYSE:ALB): 2024 loss: -40.4%. 2023 drop: -33.4%

AES (NYSE:AES): 2024 loss: -33.14%. 2023 drop: -33.07%

Etsy (NASDAQ:ETSY): 2024 loss: -34.7%. 2023 drop: -32.3%

Investors can track the S&P 500 (SP500) via exchange-traded funds that include: (VOO), (SPY), (UPRO), (IVV), and (SH).

data.title: BAC, WFC, LPLA, HOOD, KKR, ARES: Wolfe's top picks for 2025
data.description:
Wolfe Research chose Bank of America (NYSE:BAC), Wells Fargo & Company (NYSE:WFC), LPL Financial (NASDAQ:LPLA), Robinhood Markets (NASDAQ:HOOD), KKR & Co. (NYSE:KKR), Ares Management (NYSE:ARES) as its top picks for 2025.

"We believe that 2025 is shaping up to be a constructive year for the collective retail brokers, but remain selective with our preferences within the group," the analysts said in a note.

Wolfe picked LPLA as its top idea into this year, as the analysts expect a combination of higher rates/steeper curve, sweep cash growth, and best-in-class NNA to drive outperformance. Wolfe also reiterated its Outperform rating on HOOD.

"We are less constructive on the Trusts collectively, as the group is under exposed to key benefits from the Trump administration," said Wolfe.

For BAC and WFC said, Wolfe sees a number of tailwinds that support outperformance at the Money Centers.

Wolfe said it remained most constructive on both the Alternatives and Wealth Management subsectors, and added KKR, ARES were its two favorites among the Alternatives.

Wolfe Research upgraded Carlyle (NASDAQ:CG) and JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) to Outperform, and downgraded Bank of New York Mellon (NYSE:BK) & Lazard (NYSE:LAZ) to Peer Perform.

In the case of JPM, Wolfe believes the firm's NII guide is conservative, higher expense risk is well telegraphed, and share gains are unmatched.

"We prefer JPM over the Trusts as a way to play quality / defense and barbell our bank stock picks," the analysts said in a note.

For CG, Wolfe said it was growing more confident in the upside case as an improving PE backdrop should drive accelerating realization activity / increased buyback, supporting multiple convergence with peers

The brokerage firm said it continues to be positive of the BK management team, as new leadership has done an excellent job prosecuting on key initiatives, but with valuation becoming tougher to justify, downgraded it to Peer Perform.

For Lazard (LAZ), Wolfe said "while the management is clearly focused on rectifying net outflows in LAM, we are not comfortable underwriting, an improvement in the new flow outlook, which could limit comp leverage in 2025/2026."

data.title: AI stocks see green after Nvidia-Cerence announce collaboration
data.description:
AI focused stocks were in the limelight on Friday after Cerence AI (CRNC) announced an expanded collaboration with Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA).

Shares of Cerence, which provides AI powered virtual assistants for the transportation market, surged over 100% on Friday after the company said it plans to advance the capabilities of its large language models, or LLMs, called Cerence Automotive Large Language Model, or CaLLM, through Nvidia AI Enterprise.

The $3.39T market cap worth AI darling Nvidia saw its shares jump about 4% following the announcement.

The news seems to have created a buzz among other AI stocks as well, as many of them were in the green.

Microsoft (MSFT), which backs ChatGPT creator OpenAI, and Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) unit Google, the maker of chatbot Gemini, saw their shares climb 1%. Meta Platforms, which provides LLMs such as Seamless and Llama also gained about 1%.

Earlier this week, Wedbush had revealed its "top 10 tech winners for the AI Revolution in 2025," with Nvidia and Microsoft leading the list.

Companies using AI applications, such as intelligence community focused Palantir Technologies (PLTR) rose about 6%, Snowflake (SNOW) around 2%, and Pegasystems (PEGA) and Salesforce (CRM) nearly 1%, each.

E-commerce giant Amazon (AMZN), which backs Anthropic — the maker of AI model Claude — rose about 2%.

Meanwhile, iPhone maker Apple (AAPL), and creative software tools' provider Adobe (ADBE) were in the red on Friday.

data.title: Iron ore and gold: two defensive plays over the next 12-18 months amid tariffs risk
data.description:
Investors should look at iron ore (SCO:COM) and gold (GLD), (XAUUSD:CUR) as defensive buys over the next year to 18 months as the upcoming Trump administration plans to increase tariffs that could affect trade.

Tom Price, managing director of resources at Panmure Liberum, said in a CNBC interview that former President Trump has already given a playbook for commodities in 2018-2019.

He said that there were only two commodities that did really well, gold (GLD), (XAUUSD:CUR), and iron ore (SCO:COM), outperformed gold during that time.

“China freaked out and said, ‘oh, wow, tariffs, okay, we better beef up investment in our own economy,’” he said. “So, they went down the steel intensive route. So, the iron trade boomed, and so did the price.”

In 2018, iron ore’s global production saw a slight increase, driven by increased demand from China. The U.S. saw a 3% rise in domestic iron ore production that year.

According to the United States Geological Survey, global iron ore production was 2.46B metric tons of usable ore, led by Australia with about 900 metric tons, Brazil with 460 metric tons, China with 335 metric tons, India with 205 metric tons and Russia with 96.1 metric tons – all together accounting for 81% of global production.

In 2019, China’s iron ore imports rose 0.47% hovering just below their all-time annual peak.

Data from the General Administration of Customs showed that China brought in 1.069B tons of iron ore in 2019, up from 1.064B tons in 2018.

Iron ore stocks:

  • Rio Tinto Group (RIO)
  • Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF)
  • Gerdau S.A. (GGB)
  • Black Iron (OTCPK:BKIRF)
  • Steel Dynamics (STLD)

  • Fortescue (OTCQX:FSUMF)
  • BHP Group (BHP)
  • Grange Resources (OTCPK:GRRLF)
  • Champion Iron (OTCQX:CIAFF)
  • Reliance Steel & Aluminum Co. (RS)
